steps.m: Ha Meem (special letters that begin this chapter)
steps.n: These are special letters that appear at the start of some chapters in the Quran. They are simply pronounced by saying the names of each letter: 'Ha' then 'Meem'. While their exact meaning is unknown, they show something special about the Quran.
Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
Changed to a noun showing the action itself, with emphasis on it being done gradually or in stages

steps.m: the sending down (subject of this ayah)
steps.n: The ُ shows this word is the subject of the sentence, followed by 'of the Book' (الكتاب)
Changes from verb 'to write' into noun 'book'
Adds ٱلْ to make it specific ('the book') and ِ to show it belongs to تَنزِيلُ (revelation), forming 'revelation of the book'
This is the basic preposition that shows where something comes from or its source. In this ayah, it connects 'the sending down of the book' with its source (Allah).
Added fatha at the end to help connect it smoothly to the next word (Allah). This is a pronunciation helper that doesn't change the meaning.
Changed to the special form used only for Allah, with ال merged into the word
Changed to 'of Allah' by adding the kasra (small diagonal line) at the end to show it belongs to 'revelation'
Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past tense verb
Changed the pattern to show someone who has this quality very strongly
Added ال to make it specific, referring to Allah in this context

steps.m: The Mighty One (a name of Allah in this ayah, connected to الحكيم)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show connection to the next word الحكيم, both being names of Allah
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Changed the verb into an adjective describing someone who has wisdom
Added ٱلْ to make it specific and ِ to show it belongs to or describes Allah in this context

This is a special word that makes what comes after it more important. It's like saying 'Listen! There really are signs...' in English. In this ayah, it especially emphasizes that the signs (لَءَايٰتٍ) definitely exist.
This is a preposition that shows location. In this ayah, it shows that the signs/miracles (ءَايٰتٍ) are located in the heavens and earth (ٱلسَّمٰوٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ).
Added vowels to connect the letters and make a basic past-tense verb
Changed from a verb meaning 'to rise' into a noun meaning 'sky' by adding ء at the end
Added feminine plural ending ات and brought back the و before it
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the س with shadda because it comes after ال
Added kasra at the end because it comes after the word فِى (in). The alifs are written as long alifs (ٰ) in this style.
Added basic vowel marks to connect the letters together
Adding 'ال' makes it specific - this is THE earth
Adding 'و' connects this word to what came before it
The kasra at the end shows this comes after the word في (in) from earlier in the ayah: فِى ٱلسَّمٰوٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ (in the heavens and the earth)

steps.m: to show, make clear, be apparent

steps.n: Adding ة at the end makes this a feminine noun meaning 'a sign'
Changed from one sign/miracle to many signs/miracles by adding ات at the end
Added لِ (li) to show these signs are 'for' something or someone
Changed from 'li' (for/to) to 'la' (surely/indeed) and added tanween to show it's indefinite and connects to 'the believers' that follows
Adding alif at start changes meaning to 'make someone do the action'
Changed from the action of believing to describing a person who believes
Added ين to show multiple believers (masculine plural)
Added ال to mean 'the believers' specifically
Added ل with shadda to mean 'for/to the believers'. The shadda appears because two ل letters combine - one from the preposition ل and one from the definite article ال
Basic preposition showing location or containment. In this ayah, it shows what contains the signs (آيَات)

steps.m: and in (location in خَلْقِكُمْ - your creation)
steps.n: Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it

steps.m: to create, form, or shape something from nothing

steps.n: Changed the verb (to create) into a noun (creation) by adding vowel marks. Like changing 'create' to 'creating' in English.
Added ِكُمْ at the end to show this belongs to multiple people being spoken to. The ِ connects it to the previous word.
Basic word meaning 'what' or 'whatever' - here it refers to the creatures that spread on earth
Added و at the beginning to connect this phrase to what came before it. This و connects this sign (the spreading of creatures) to the previous sign mentioned (your creation)

steps.m: to spread or scatter things around

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Changed to a command form and combined the repeated ث with a shadda mark
Added يَ at the beginning to change it from a command to 'he' doing the action in present/future tense
Basic preposition showing where something comes from or what it belongs to. Here it connects what Allah spreads (يَبُثُّ) with the creatures (دَآبَّةٍ) being spread.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Changed to noun meaning 'one that moves' and made it feminine
Added genitive case ending (kasratan) to show it's affected by a preposition

steps.m: a creature (with emphasis on continuous movement)
steps.n: Added long alif to emphasize the continuous nature of the creature's movement
Changes the root letters into a noun meaning 'a sign' by adding an alif at start and taa at end
Adds the two dots (tanween) at the end to show this is indefinite and in the subject position
The root meaning of standing together becomes a word for a group of people who stand as one. This word is owned by آيات (signs) in this ayah.
Added لِ at the beginning to show these signs are 'for' the people
Added shadda on ل for emphasis and tanwin at the end to show it means 'a people' rather than specific people. These are the people who are certain (يُوقِنُونَ) mentioned next.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the past tense verb
Changes from past to present tense by adding 'yu' at start, meaning ongoing certainty

steps.m: they are becoming/remaining certain (referring to people who reflect on Allah's signs)
steps.n: The word changed from 'he' to 'they' by adding ون at the end - now it means multiple people are certain, not just one person
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Added ٱ at start and ت in middle to show things becoming different from each other (like people disagreeing)
Changed to a noun showing the act of differing by adding ا after second root letter
Added و at beginning to connect this word to what came before it
Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to 'the night and day' that follows it
Added vowel marks to connect the letters together and make a basic noun
Added 'the' at the beginning with a shadda on the first letter because it combines with the ل of the word

steps.m: of the night (part of وَٱخْتِلٰفِ ٱلَّيْلِ - the alternation of the night)
steps.n: Added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to the word before it (وَٱخْتِلٰفِ - alternation)

steps.m: to flow brightly or shine (like daylight)

steps.n: Forms a noun for the time when light flows/shines
Adds the definite article 'the' and doubles the ن because it's a sun letter
Adds 'and' at the beginning and changes اَل to ٱل

steps.m: and the daytime (possessed by/connected to what follows)
steps.n: Shows this word is connected to what follows it in the sentence
This is a particle meaning 'what' - it has no root
Added conjunction 'and' (و) at beginning
Lengthened the final alif for emphasis and pronunciation
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Adding أَ at the start makes someone cause the action instead of doing it directly. Here Allah is causing something to descend (sending it down).

steps.m: a deity, one who is worshipped
steps.n: Changed the root into a noun for 'deity' by adding vowel marks
Added the u-mark at the end to show it's in the nominative case
Added 'the' to make it specific/definite
This is a basic preposition meaning 'from' that shows where something starts or comes from
Added fatha at the end to connect it smoothly to the next word السَّمَآءِ (the sky/heaven). This combination means 'from the sky/heaven'

steps.m: to rise up or be elevated

steps.n: Adds اء to make it a noun referring to something high up
Doubles the middle letter to add emphasis to the meaning

steps.m: the sky (possessed by or following something)
steps.n: Added ال (the) at the start and changed the ending to show it comes after the word 'from' (مِنَ) in the sentence
This is a preposition meaning 'from' that connects what Allah sent down (أَنزَلَ) with the provision/sustenance (رِّزْقٍ). It shows where the provision comes from.
Changed from basic root meaning 'provide' into the noun 'provision' by adding the vowel 'i' after first letter and making middle letter silent
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning
Added tanween kasra to show this is indefinite and is the object of the preposition مِن (from)
Added vowel marks to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb
The أ at the start makes it mean 'cause to live' instead of just 'live'. This is a verb, so it doesn't have nominative, genitive or accusative case.

steps.m: and then he gave life (to الْأَرْضَ/the earth)
steps.n: The فَ (fa) was added at the start to mean 'and then/so', connecting it to what happened before. The word shows what Allah gave life to (the earth)
Added kasra (ِ) to the letter ب to show it's a preposition meaning 'with'
Added the pronoun ه (him/it) with kasra to show what the preposition connects to. Here it refers back to the provision (رِّزْقٍ) mentioned earlier in the verse.
Added basic vowel marks to connect the letters

steps.m: the earth (object being brought to life in فَأَحْيَا)
steps.n: Added ال to make it specific ('the earth') and fatḥa at the end shows it's receiving the action of being brought to life in the phrase فَأَحْيَا (and He brought to life)

steps.m: to be far away from something

steps.n: Root letters ب-ع-د (meaning 'far') were joined together with vowels to make a word meaning 'after'
Added fatha at the end to show this word connects to what comes after it (its death)
Adding vowel marks to make the basic noun form
Added kasra to show possession relationship

steps.m: its death (referring to الْأَرْضَ - the earth)
steps.n: Added ها to show possession by the earth (which is feminine in Arabic)
Connected the root letters with fatha to form basic past tense verb
Changes the verb 'he turned' into a noun meaning 'turning repeatedly' - like going from 'turn' to 'turning over and over'
Added 'and' at beginning and made it genitive because it's connected to what comes after it (the winds). This word is part of a list of signs from Allah mentioned in this ayah.
Changed و to ي and added kasra to make it mean the thing that moves through space (wind)
Added alif to make it plural (many winds)
Added ال (the) at start and kasra at end because it's connected to the word before it (وَتَصْرِيفِ)
Changes from verb 'to show' into plural noun meaning 'signs'
The word is already plural (meaning 'signs'). The main difference is just in how it's written - with a different type of alif and without the end marks.

steps.m: signs, miracles (subject of this part of the verse)
steps.n: Added double damma to show this is the subject doing something in this part of the verse

steps.m: to stand firmly or rise up

steps.n: Changes verb root into a noun meaning a group of people

steps.m: for a people (who use their minds)
steps.n: Added لِ at beginning to mean 'for' and tanween at end to make it indefinite ('a' instead of 'the')

steps.m: for a people (who use their minds)
steps.n: Added shadda on ل because it comes after a word ending in sukun
Connected the root letters with fatha to make the past tense form
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: they use reason (referring to those who reflect on Allah's signs)
steps.n: Added ونَ to show multiple people are doing the action. In this ayah, it refers to those who reflect on Allah's signs in nature.
This is the base form of the feminine demonstrative pronoun meaning 'that'

steps.m: that (feminine, pointing to something far away) (refers to ءَايٰتُ)
steps.n: Adding كَ at the end shows we're pointing to something far away. In this ayah, it's pointing to ءَايٰتُ (signs/verses) that will be mentioned.

steps.m: to show, appear, or be clear

steps.n: Adding ة at the end turns this into a noun meaning 'sign'
Changes the word to show many signs instead of just one
Adds ُ to show this word is the subject being read in the phrase 'We recite them (the signs)'
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity'
Added ال with special spelling (ٱل) to make it definite, doubled the ل with shadda, and added kasra at the end to show this word is connected to what comes after it in the sentence
Connected the root letters with fatha marks. The final و changes to ا because it's a weak letter at the end of a past tense verb.
The prefix نَ shows 'we' are doing the action. The vowels change to show present tense, and the final و shows it's present tense with a weak letter.
The suffix هَا means 'it' (feminine) and refers back to ءَايٰتُ (verses) mentioned earlier in the ayah.
This is the basic preposition meaning 'on' or 'upon'. It's one of the most common prepositions in Arabic.
When adding a pronoun after عَلَى, the ى changes to يْ to make it easier to connect with what comes next

steps.m: upon you (being addressed in نَتْلُوهَا - we recite it)
steps.n: Added كَ at the end to show this is happening to 'you'. This word connects to نَتْلُوهَا (we recite it) to show we are reciting it to you.
The shadda (ّ) on the ق shows emphasis, making it mean absolute or definite truth

steps.m: with the truth/reality (نَتْلُوهَا - we recite it)
steps.n: Added بِ at the beginning to show this truth is being used as a tool or means. This word relates to نَتْلُوهَا (we recite it) in the ayah, showing how the recitation is done.
Combined the letters with fatha and shadda to make the question word
Added kasra to show this word is being acted upon by the preposition بِ
Added the preposition بِ to show 'by means of' or 'with'
Added فَ to connect this question with the previous statement about Allah's signs being recited

steps.m: to tell, to speak, to narrate

steps.n: Changes from verb 'to speak' into noun 'speech'

steps.m: a speech/narrative (that comes after اللَّهِ وَءَايٰتِهِۦ)
steps.n: Added the tanween kasra (ٍ) to show this is any speech/narrative (not a specific one) and it's connected to what comes after it in the ayah
Added vowel marks to transform the root into a word meaning 'after'. This word is connected to what comes after it in the ayah (Allah and His signs).
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity' by adding specific vowel patterns

This is the unique name for the one true God in Islam. The ال makes it definite (like 'the'), the shadda emphasizes it, and the kasra at the end shows it's being acted upon by something else in the sentence.

steps.m: to show, make clear, or be evident

steps.n: Adding ة changes this into a feminine noun meaning 'sign'
Changes from singular to plural by adding ات and changing the middle vowel

steps.m: and signs, miracles, or clear proofs
steps.n: Adds و at beginning to connect with previous phrase

steps.m: and signs, miracles, or clear proofs (possessed)
steps.n: Adds kasra to show this word is possessed by something
Adds هِ to show these are Allah's signs specifically
Adding long alif at start changes meaning to 'make someone do the action'
Changes past tense to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels
The ون ending shows that a group of people (all male or mixed) are doing the action
This is a fixed expression word used to warn of severe consequences or express a threat. It has no root since it's a complete word by itself.

The ٌ (tanween damma) shows this word is the subject doing the action in the sentence. It's followed by لِّكُلِّ أَفَّاكٍ أَثِيمٍ which means 'for every sinful liar', showing who the woe/destruction is for.
Added damma and doubled the middle letter to create a word meaning 'every' or 'all'. This word is connected to وَيْلٌ (woe) in the ayah.
Added لِ at the beginning to show 'for/to' and kasra at the end because it's being acted upon by the لِ
Added shadda on the ل to emphasize the meaning
Added short vowel marks (fat-ha) to make it mean 'he lied' in the past tense
Added shadda on middle letter and alif after it to show someone who lies habitually
Added tanween kasra to show this describes 'each/every' (كُلِّ) in the phrase 'woe to every constant liar'

steps.m: to do wrong, to sin, to be guilty

steps.n: Added ي between second and third root letters with vowels to show someone who regularly does this action. This word describes أَفَّاكٍ (great liar) in this ayah.
Added tanween kasra (ٍ) to show this is indefinite, like saying 'a' sinner rather than 'the' sinner
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Added يَ at start to show present tense, changed middle letter to sukun, and added ُ at end to show it's happening now. This word refers to hearing the verses (آيَات) of Allah being recited.

steps.m: to show, make clear, be evident

steps.n: Changed أ to ء and added long vowel alif to create a noun meaning 'something that shows or proves'
Changed regular alif to extended alif (alif maqsurah) for writing style
Added feminine plural ending to show multiple signs
Added kasra to show this word belongs to Allah in the phrase 'signs of Allah'
Added ال to specify this is THE one true deity, and the hamza was dropped because this is a special divine name

Added shadda on ل, changed initial alif to wasl form, and added kasra at end to show this word belongs to or describes something else. In this ayah, it shows these are Allah's signs/verses being mentioned.

steps.m: to recite or follow along with words

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form basic past tense verb
Changed from 'he recited' to 'being recited' by adding تُ at start and changing vowels to make it passive

steps.m: it is being recited (verses of ءَايٰتِ ٱللَّهِ)
steps.n: Changed final weak letter و to ى with alif khanjariyah (ٰ) due to being at end of word
This is the basic preposition meaning 'on' or 'upon'
The ى changes to ي with sukun when adding a pronoun
Added هِ at the end to show this means 'upon him'. This word connects to تُتْلَىٰ (recited) in this ayah, showing Allah's verses are being recited to him.

steps.m: then (after hearing Allah's verses being recited to him)
steps.n: This word connects two actions, showing that one happens after the other. In this ayah, it shows what happens after someone hears Allah's verses being recited.
Combined the repeated ر into one letter with shadda to show emphasis

steps.m: he persists stubbornly (subject of يَسْمَعُ - he hears)
steps.n: Changed to present tense by adding يُ at start and changing vowels. This shows ongoing stubborn persistence after hearing Allah's signs being recited.
Added vowels to make a basic past tense verb
The مست pattern shows someone making themselves something. Here it means someone making themselves seem greater than others.

steps.m: arrogantly (describes how he persists in يُصِرُّ)
steps.n: The ًا ending shows this word describes how the action يُصِرُّ (persists) is done - he persists arrogantly after hearing Allah's verses.
This is a basic comparison particle that means 'like' or 'similar to'

steps.m: as if (he did not hear it)
steps.n: Adding أَن changes 'like' to 'as if', making it introduce a hypothetical situation. In this ayah, it describes someone acting as if they didn't hear Allah's verses being recited.
This is the basic negative particle that makes present tense verbs mean they didn't happen in the past
Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger and more emphatic
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Added يَ at start to show present tense, and changed vowels to show it's conditional (because it comes after لَمْ in the ayah)

steps.m: he hears it (referring to ءَايٰتِ - verses)
steps.n: Added هَا to show what is being heard - refers back to ءَايٰتِ (verses) mentioned earlier in the ayah
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
The shadda doubles the middle letter to make the verb stronger and turns it into a command
Added pronoun هُ (him) to show who should receive the announcement

steps.m: so announce to him! (as a command)
steps.n: Added فَ (so/then) to connect this command with what came before it in the verse
Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Long ا after second root letter makes this a noun meaning the result of punishment. The word is in genitive case because it comes after the preposition ب (with/by).
Added بِ meaning 'with' at the start and tanween ٍ at the end to show it's indefinite (like 'a/an' in English). This word is connected to فَبَشِّرْهُ (then give him news of) in the ayah.
Added short vowels (fat-ha, kasra, fat-ha) to make 'he felt pain' in past tense
Added ي to make an adjective meaning 'causing intense pain'
Added tanween kasra to show this word describes the punishment (عَذَابٍ) mentioned in the ayah

This is a conditional particle that introduces a time-based condition, similar to saying 'whenever' or 'at the time when' in English. In this ayah, it connects to عَلِمَ (he knew/learned) to show what happens at that time.

steps.m: and when (connects this conditional clause to what came before)
steps.n: Added وَ at the beginning to connect this condition to previous statements
Added vowels to show this is about someone (he) knowing something in the past. In this ayah, it refers to knowing something from Allah's signs (ءَايٰتِنَا).
This is a basic preposition that shows where something comes from or starts. In this ayah, it's used with ءَايٰتِنَا (our signs/verses) to mean 'some of our signs'

steps.m: to show a sign or mark

steps.n: Adding ة makes this a feminine noun referring to a single sign or verse
Changes from singular 'sign' to plural 'signs'
Signs (plural) - written with a long alif (ٰ) instead of regular alif
Added نَا to show possession (our) and ِ because it comes after the preposition مِنْ
Connected the root letters and added vowel marks to make the basic word for 'thing'. The hamza at the end is part of the root.
Added tanween fatha to show this is the object of the sentence
Added basic past tense vowels to connect the root letters
Changed to show this is something done for oneself - 'took for himself'
Added هَا to show what was taken (referring to آيَاتِنَا - our signs), and changed initial alif to waṣla form. This word connects to هُزُوًا (mockery) in the ayah.
The damma vowels (ُ) change this from a verb into a noun meaning mockery
The hamza (ء) changes to و because it's easier to pronounce
Added ًا at the end to show this is the object being taken/used in the phrase 'took it as mockery'
This is the basic form of the word used to point to things or people that are far away
The alif with line (ٰ) shows that these things are far away
Adding ئِ makes it more specific, like pointing with your finger while saying 'those ones there'
The كَ at the end makes it even more specific about who we're pointing to. In this ayah, it refers to the people who mock Allah's signs mentioned in the previous ayah.
Added fatha to help with pronunciation

steps.m: for them (who will have عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ)
steps.n: Added هُمْ at the end to show who the preposition applies to. This word connects to عَذَابٌ مُّهِينٌ (humiliating punishment) which comes right after it in the ayah.
Adding an alif between the second and third root letters and fathas on the first two letters turns this into a noun that names the result of the action.
Adding double damma (tanween) shows this is indefinite (like saying 'a punishment' instead of 'the punishment'). This word is the subject that goes with لَهُمْ (for them) in the ayah.

steps.m: to make light of something or treat it as insignificant

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters together
Added مُ at start and changed vowels to make it mean 'someone who humiliates' instead of 'to humiliate'
Added shadda to emphasize the meaning
Added tanween to show this describes the punishment (عَذَابٌ) mentioned in the ayah
Basic preposition showing where something starts or comes from
Added shadda because this word connects to the word after it. In this ayah, it connects to وَرَآئِهِمْ (behind them) to show what is 'from behind them'
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and make them readable
Added hamza at the end to complete the word
The word 'behind' gets a long alif and hamza with kasra to connect to what comes next

steps.m: behind them (referring to the disbelievers mentioned earlier)
steps.n: Added هِمْ to show this is 'behind them'. The 'them' refers to the disbelievers mentioned earlier in the surah.
A proper noun for the place of punishment in the afterlife

steps.m: Hell (as the subject of what comes after)
steps.n: Added the ُ mark at the end to show this word is the subject of what follows in the verse
This is the basic negative particle that negates what comes after it
Added و at the beginning to connect this negation with what came before. This word negates the verb يُغْنِى (benefit/avail) in this ayah.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb

steps.m: he makes sufficient (subject of this verb is Allah)
steps.n: Added يُ at the beginning to show present tense and make it causative (making something happen). The ى at the end shows it's a weak verb. In this ayah, Allah is making something sufficient.

steps.m: away from, about (preposition showing separation or topic)
steps.n: This is a preposition that shows movement away from something or indicates the topic being discussed

steps.m: away from them/about them (object of يُغْنِى - 'will not avail')
steps.n: The هم suffix refers to a group of three or more males (or mixed gender group). In this ayah, it refers back to the disbelievers who are the subject of the passage, showing that their deeds will not benefit them.
This is a relative pronoun that connects to كَسَبُوا۟ (they earned) to mean 'what they earned'
The shadda adds emphasis to show importance of what follows
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Added وا at the end to show that a group of people (they) did the action
Connected the root letters to make the common noun meaning 'thing'. The hamza at the end makes a glottal stop sound.
Added the accusative case ending because this word is the object of يُغْنِى (avail/benefit). The hamza changes position due to the added ending.
This is a negative particle that negates the verb يُغْنِى (it benefits) that comes after it in the ayah
The و connects this negation to what came before, showing these are additional things that won't benefit them

steps.m: that which (refers to what they earned)
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun connecting to كَسَبُوا (they earned) in this ayah. It introduces what was earned.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
The ت is doubled (has shadda) to show this is done with more effort/purpose than just 'taking'
Added وا to show multiple people did this action, and changed the first alif to a waṣla form since it's not needed for pronunciation when connected to previous words
Basic preposition that shows something comes from somewhere else or belongs to something. Here it connects to the word دُونِ (besides/other than) to show what something is from/of.
Added kasra (ِ) under the م to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'min'
This is a special word that shows something is less than, below, or separate from something else
Adding the damma (ُ) mark changes how we say the word but keeps the same meaning
Added kasra at the end because this word is connected to Allah (اللَّهِ) that comes after it, showing it means 'besides Allah' or 'other than Allah'
Added ال and merged it with the word, changing إ to ل to create the unique name Allah
Added kasra at the end to show possession, shadda on ل for emphasis, and changed initial alif to wasl form since it comes after another word
Added vowels and doubled the final letter to show intensity of the quality
Changed from singular to plural by adding أ at start and changing ending to اء - this is a broken plural pattern
Added fatha at end to show this is the object of the verb اتَّخَذُوا (they took)

steps.m: and for them (connected to عَذَابٌ - punishment)
steps.n: Adds 'them' to show who the punishment is for. This word connects to عَذَابٌ (punishment) in the next part of the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past-tense verb
Adding an alif between the second and third root letters turns this into a noun that means the result of the action. This word is described by the adjective عَظِيمٌ (mighty) that comes after it.
Adding the double dammah makes this an indefinite noun - meaning 'a punishment' rather than 'the punishment'
Adding the 'ee' sound (ي) in the middle makes this describe something as having this quality very strongly
Adding the two dots above (ٌ) makes this word indefinite, meaning 'a great' instead of 'the great'

Adding ذَ makes it point to something specific and nearby
Adding ا at the end makes it a complete demonstrative pronoun for masculine singular things that are nearby
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb

steps.m: guidance (this word is the subject of هٰذَا/this)
steps.n: Changed first vowel to damma and final ي to ى to make it a noun meaning the concept of guidance. This word is what هٰذَا (this) is referring to in the ayah.
Added tanween (double fatha) to show this is an indefinite noun meaning 'a guidance'
This is a relative pronoun that doesn't come from a root. It connects descriptions to the things they describe.

Changed to plural form by adding ين ending

Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Added damma (ُ) at the end to show this is the subject of the sentence
Added وا to show this is a group of people (3 or more). This word is the subject of لَهُمْ (for them) in this ayah.

steps.m: to show, make clear, or be apparent

steps.n: Adding ة at the end turns this into a noun meaning 'sign'
Added plural ending ات to show multiple signs
Added preposition بِ to show how these signs are used or related to the action
Added kasra at the end because this word is connected to رَبِّهِمْ (their Lord) in the phrase 'signs of their Lord'

steps.m: to care for, nurture, or develop something to its full potential
lord (one who nurtures and sustains completely)
steps.n: The doubled middle letter shows this is someone who nurtures and takes care of others perfectly
Adding kasra at the end makes this a call/cry to one's Lord, like saying 'O my Lord!'
The هِمْ shows this belongs to them. This word connects to عَذَابٌ (punishment) in the ayah, showing they will receive punishment from their Lord.
Added fatha to show this means 'to/for' rather than other meanings of ل
Added هُمْ which means 'them' to show who is being referred to. This connects to the people mentioned earlier who disbelieved (كَفَرُوا).
Adding an alif between the second and third root letters and fathas on the first two letters turns this into a noun that names the result of the action.

steps.m: a punishment (subject of لَهُمْ - they have)
steps.n: Adding double damma (tanween) shows this is indefinite (like saying 'a punishment' instead of 'the punishment'). This word is what they have (لَهُمْ) in this verse.

The dot under the م gets a little line (shadda) on top because it connects smoothly with the word before it. It's like saying 'from what kind' of punishment instead of just 'from'.
The shadda emphasizes the punishment, showing it's severe. This word is part of عَذَابٌ مِّن رِّجْزٍ (a punishment of severe torment).
The word gets tanween (double kasra) to show it's describing the punishment (عَذَابٌ) mentioned earlier - like saying 'a filthy punishment'
Added ي between second and last root letter with vowels to create an intensive adjective pattern. This word describes عَذَابٌ (punishment) in the ayah.
Added double dammah to make the word indefinite - now means 'an intensely painful (punishment)'
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'a deity'
This is the unique name for the one true God in Islam. The ال makes it definite and the shadda emphasizes the L sound. The ُ shows this word is the subject of the sentence.
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that connects descriptions to what they're describing
Added ال with shadda to make it specific. In this ayah, it refers back to Allah (اللَّهُ) and connects to what comes after - that He is the one who made the sea useful for ships.

steps.m: to make something serve or submit

steps.n: Adding basic past tense vowels

steps.m: he thoroughly made (the sea) serve (for your benefit)
steps.n: Adding shadda makes the meaning stronger, showing Allah completely and thoroughly made the sea serviceable for humans. This word relates to البَحْرَ (the sea) which comes after it in the ayah.

steps.m: for you all (connects to سَخَّرَ - subjected)
steps.n: Added كُمُ to show this is directed at multiple people. This word connects to سَخَّرَ (subjected) showing what was subjected for you all.

steps.n: Added vowel marks to make the noun form. This word means sea/ocean because it refers to a large body of water that cuts through the land.

Added َ at the end to show this is the object that Allah has subjected/made subservient in the phrase سَخَّرَ لَكُمُ ٱلْبَحْرَ (He subjected for you the sea)

steps.m: so that it (feminine) may flow (referring to ٱلْفُلْكُ - the ship)

steps.m: to orbit, circle, or move in a circular path
ship (because of its rounded hull and circular motion)

The ى changes to يْ to prepare for adding a pronoun suffix
Adding كُم shows this applies to multiple people being addressed
Added fatha and doubled the middle letter to make a noun meaning 'truth'
Added ال to show we're talking about a specific truth
Added بِ to show this truth is being used as a tool or method (Our Book speaks against you with/by the truth)
Added kasra at the end because this word is the object that receives the action of 'speaking' (يَنطِقُ)
Adding shadda changes this from a conditional word (if) to an emphasis word (indeed)
Adding ا at the end shows this is about 'we'. This word connects to كُنَّا (we were) later in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the basic past tense verb
The middle weak letter و drops out, and vowels adjust accordingly
Adding نا at the end makes it mean 'we'. The ن gets a shadda for emphasis. This word is the subject (doer) of نَسْتَنسِخُ (we record/copy) in this ayah.

steps.m: to record, to copy, to write down

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters into a basic past tense verb

steps.m: we record/copy (what كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ - you used to do)
steps.n: Added 'ست' to show 'we' are doing the action, changed vowels to show it's happening now (present tense)

steps.m: what (that which you used to do)
steps.n: This is a relative pronoun that connects to تَعْمَلُونَ (you used to do) in this ayah. It refers back to the actions being recorded in the book mentioned earlier.
Connected the root letters and added basic vowels to form the noun of being
Added تَ at the end to show 'you' in the past tense. The و is dropped because it's a weak letter in past tense verbs.
Changed تَ to تُمْ to show we're talking to multiple people instead of just one person
Changed from basic root to past tense by adding the vowels 'a' after each letter
Changed to present tense with 'you' as subject

steps.m: you all work/act/do (referring to the disbelievers being addressed)
steps.n: Makes the verb plural masculine, referring to multiple people being addressed in this ayah
Starting with the basic particle أم which comes from the concept of something being in front or at the source
Adding a double letter (shadda) to أم makes it stronger, like saying 'really' or 'truly'
Adding an alif at the end changes this word from a basic emphasis word to mean 'as for' when starting a new topic

steps.m: then as for (connects to previous statement)
steps.n: Adding فَ at the beginning connects this to what came before, like saying 'then' or 'so'
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun that connects descriptions to people or things
Added ن to make it refer to multiple people (those who believed)
Added ال with shadda to make it specific and point to particular people - in this case, those who believed and did good deeds
The root conveys the basic concept of security and trust
Connected the letters together with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Adding the long alif changes the meaning to actively choosing to believe
The وا at the end shows that a group of people did this action. This word is connected to فَيُدْخِلُهُمْ (then He will admit them) in the ayah, showing who will be admitted.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make a past tense verb
Added وا to show multiple people did the action

steps.m: and they did/worked (connected to ءَامَنُوا۟ - believed)
steps.n: Added و at beginning to connect this action with 'believed' from the previous word, showing these people both believed and did good works
Connected the root letters with fatha to make a basic past tense verb
Changes from 'became good' (verb) to 'good person' (describes someone). Like changing 'ran' to 'runner'.
Changed from 'righteous person' to 'good deeds' by adding -aat ending to make it plural. Shows what good things they did in the sentence.
Added definite article
Added kasra at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb عَمِلُوا (they did/performed)
Basic meaning of entering
Connected the root letters with fatha to make past tense verb
Added يُ at start and changed vowels to make it causative present tense - meaning someone causes the action to happen
Added هُمْ to show who is being made to enter

steps.m: so he makes them enter (into رَحْمَتِهِ)
steps.n: Added فَ to show this happens as a result of what came before. In this ayah, they are being made to enter into Allah's mercy.
Root meaning of nurturing or sustaining
Doubling the ب shows this is someone who does a lot of nurturing/caring
Added damma at the end to show this is the subject doing the action in the sentence
Added هُمْ at the end to show this is their Lord. This Lord is the one who will admit them (يُدْخِلُ) into His mercy.
Basic preposition showing location or containment. Here it connects to 'His mercy' to show they will be placed in His mercy.
The ي changes to ى at the end of words in Arabic writing
Connected the root letters with fatha on first letter and sukun on middle letter to create the base noun
Adding ة makes it mean 'one specific act of mercy' instead of general 'mercy'
Added 'ت' and kasra at the end to show this mercy belongs to someone
Added هِ to show it belongs to Him (their Lord in this ayah)
Starting with the basic word for 'this' (ذا) and adding the alif above (ٰ) to show distance
Added ل with kasra (لِ) to make the word stronger and more emphatic
Added ك with fatha (كَ) at the end to show we're pointing to something far away
This is a basic pronoun that points back to ذٰلِكَ (that) which refers to entering into Allah's mercy. The ُ (damma) and َ (fatha) help show it's a separate word, not attached to anything else.
Basic meaning of achieving success or victory
Changes from basic meaning into a noun meaning 'the act of succeeding'
Added damma at the end to show this is the main subject being discussed
Added the definite article 'the' to specify this particular success, which is described as clear/manifest in the ayah
Root meaning of making something clear or evident
Added short vowels (fat-ha) to make it mean 'he made clear' in the past tense
Changed to active participle form showing who/what does the action
Added the definite article ال and nominative case ending ُ. This word describes هُوَ (he) in the phrase ذٰلِكَ هُوَ ٱلْفَوْزُ ٱلْمُبِينُ (that is the clear success)
This is a basic particle that introduces a new topic or contrast. It's often used at the start of a sentence to shift focus to something new.

steps.m: and as for (introduces contrast with previous statement)
steps.n: Adding وَ (and) at the beginning connects this to the previous statement while maintaining the contrast. In this ayah, it introduces what happens to those who disbelieve, contrasting with a previous statement about believers.
Added ال with a shadda to make it specific. This word connects descriptions to people or things, like saying 'the one who' or 'that which' in English.
Added ين at the end to make it plural. This shows we're talking about multiple people. In this ayah, it refers to the people who disbelieved (كَفَرُوٓا۟).

steps.m: to reject, to cover up, to be ungrateful

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make it a past tense verb
Added damma (ُ) at the end to show this is the subject of the sentence

steps.m: they rejected/disbelieved (those who are mentioned in الَّذِينَ)
steps.n: Added وا to show multiple people did this action. This verb tells us what 'those who' (الَّذِينَ) did - they disbelieved.
The أ at the beginning makes this a question, showing disbelief or surprise
The فَ shows this follows from what came before (the previous ayah talking about those who disbelieved)

steps.m: Then did not (questioning the past negation)
steps.n: The لَمْ makes this negative and refers to the past. In this ayah, it leads into 'were not My verses recited to you?'
Added تَ at beginning to show 'you' are doing the action

steps.m: you are (in the phrase 'were not my verses...')
steps.n: Added sukun at end because this is part of a question about the past ('were not my verses...'). The و is dropped because of the sukun.
Forms the basic noun for a single sign by changing the root letters and adding ة at the end
Forms regular feminine plural by adding ات suffix. This word is the subject of the verb تكن (to be) in the negative past tense question 'were not my signs...?'
The word changes from 'my signs' to just 'signs' when connected to another word. Like going from 'my book' to 'the book of...'
The ى at the end shows it belongs to someone (like 'my' in English)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks. The و changes to ا at the end because it's a weak letter.

steps.m: it is being recited (referring to ءَايٰتِى/My signs)
steps.n: Changed from 'he recited' to 'being recited' by adding تُ at the start and changing the middle vowel - like changing from active to passive in English. The ى at the end shows it's happening now.
Added alif khanjariyah (ٰ) which is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning
This is the basic preposition meaning 'on' or 'upon'. It's one of the most common prepositions in Arabic.
The ى changes to يْ when adding a suffix. This is just a spelling change that doesn't affect the meaning.
Added كُمْ at the end to show this applies to multiple people being spoken to. This appears in an ayah where Allah is addressing the disbelievers about His signs being recited to them.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make it a past tense verb
Added است at beginning to show someone trying to make themselves seem greater than they are
Added تُمْ to show that multiple people (you all) did this action in the past
Added فَ at beginning to show this happened as a result of something before it
Changed root letters into a command by adding damma (u) and sukun (stop mark) - means 'Be!'
Added تُ (tu) at the end to change from a command to 'I was' in past tense
Changed from 'I was' to 'you all were' by adding مْ at the end
Added و with fatha at beginning to mean 'and'
Changes verb root into a noun meaning 'a group that stands together'
Added tanween alif to show this is the predicate of كُنتُمْ (you were). In Arabic, when you use كان or words like it (such as كُنتُمْ), the predicate takes this special ending.
Connected the root letters with fatha to make it a past tense verb
Adding مُ at the start and changing the vowels turns this into a noun for someone who does the action
Added emphasis (shadda) on the م for pronunciation
Added ين to show multiple criminals. This word describes قَوْمًا (people/folk) in the ayah.
This is a combination of the conjunction 'and' (و) with the conditional particle 'when' (إذا)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changed the vowels and middle letter to show this happened to something rather than someone doing it. This introduces what comes next (إِنَّ) in the ayah.
This is the same word as before - no change needed since it's already in passive form
This is the basic conditional particle
Adding shadda changes this from a conditional particle to an emphatic particle that adds certainty to what follows. Here it emphasizes Allah's promise (وَعْدَ ٱللَّهِ) that comes after it.

steps.m: to make a promise or commitment

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the past tense verb
Changed to a noun form by adding sukun on middle letter. This word is the object of إِنَّ (indeed/certainly) in this ayah.
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ل, transforming it from 'a god' to 'The God' (Allah)
Added kasra at the end to show possession of the word وَعْدَ (promise) that comes before it in the ayah. Also added shadda on ل and changed initial alif to hamzat wasl.
Added fatha and doubled the final letter to create a noun meaning the essence of being true
Added tanween damma to show this is the subject of the phrase 'Indeed, Allah's promise is truth'

steps.m: to move or pass through time

steps.n: Changed the root into a noun meaning a specific period of time
Added ال to make it specific. The ل causes the س to double (shown by ّ)

steps.m: and the Hour (referring to Judgment Day)
steps.n: Added و (meaning 'and') at the start and ُ at the end to show this is the subject. The ال changes to ٱل after و
This is a negative particle that negates what comes after it. In this ayah, it negates رَيْبَ (doubt), making the phrase mean 'there is no doubt'
Added fatha mark to show the correct pronunciation as 'laa'
Added vowel marks to turn the root into a noun. The َ (fatha) on ر and ْ (sukun) on ي create a noun pattern.
The َ (fatha) at the end shows this word is affected by لا (no/not). When لا is used to completely negate something (meaning 'there is no...'), it puts the noun after it in this special form.
This is a basic preposition showing location or containment
Added the feminine pronoun ها to show what is being referred to. Here it refers back to الساعة (the Hour) which is feminine.
Added damma and sukun to make the basic command form
The middle letter و is removed to make the word shorter and easier to say as a command
Adding تُ at the end changes it from a command to 'I did it'

steps.m: you all said (in response to being told about Allah's promise and the Hour)
steps.n: Added م with damma to show multiple people. This word appears in the Quran as part of the disbelievers' response when they were told about Allah's promise and the Hour.
This is a question word used to ask about things (not people)
The shadda (ّ) adds emphasis to show this is a strong question about what the Hour (Day of Judgment) is. This connects to السَّاعَةُ (the Hour) in the ayah.
Added sukun on first letter and kasra on second letter to prepare for the present tense form

steps.m: we know (in response to قِيلَ - it was said)
steps.n: Added نَ at beginning to show 'we' are doing the action. This is part of the disbelievers' response when they were told about the Hour.
This is a negative particle that makes the verb after it negative, like adding 'not' in English. Here it makes نَدْرِى (we know) into 'we do not know'
The root letters were connected with vowels, and the middle letter و changed to ا to make it easier to say. This changes it from the basic idea of measuring time to showing it already happened.

steps.m: an hour, a period of time
steps.n: Added ة to make it a specific instance of time
Added ٱل to make it specific and doubled the س with shadda to show it's connected to ٱل

steps.m: the Hour (subject of لَا رَيْبَ فِيهَا)
steps.n: Added ُ to show this word is the subject being described by لَا رَيْبَ فِيهَا (there is no doubt in it)
This is a negative particle that makes the verb after it negative. In this ayah, it negates نَّظُنُّ (we assume), showing that they are NOT assuming.

steps.m: to think or believe without being certain

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha to make past tense verb
Changed from past tense 'he thought' to present tense 'think' by adding damma marks and combining the two ن letters with a shadda
Added ن at beginning to show 'we' are doing the action
Added shadda on initial ن for emphasis
This is the base form of the exception particle that means 'except'
Adding the shadda and alif strengthens the meaning. In this ayah, it restricts the meaning of ظَنًّا (assumption) to show they only have assumptions, nothing else.
Combined the double letters with a shadda to make it a noun
Added tanween alif to show this is the object being assumed. This word is what is being assumed in the phrase 'we only assume' (نَّظُنُّ)
Basic negative particle ما with fatha added. In this ayah, it's used to negate the word مُسْتَيْقِنِينَ (certain), making the meaning 'not certain'.
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this phrase with what came before it, like saying 'and' in English.
This is the basic form of the personal pronoun meaning 'we'
Added damma (ُ) at the end to show this is the subject of the sentence
Added vowels to connect the root letters
The مُسْتَ pattern shows someone seeking or having a quality
The ين ending changes the word from 'one who is certain' to 'those who are certain' (makes it plural)
The بِ means 'with' or 'among'
Basic past tense verb with fatha vowels. This shows what became apparent to those who were mocking (يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ)

steps.m: and he became apparent (subject of يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ)
steps.n: The وَ at the beginning connects this to what happened before, showing these events happened in sequence
Added fatha to show how the letter is pronounced

steps.m: for them/to them (receiving what became apparent in بَدَا)
steps.n: Added هُمْ (them) to show who the action is for. This word connects to بَدَا (became apparent) to show who something became apparent to.
Changed from a root meaning 'being evil' into a noun meaning 'evil thing' by adding vowel marks
The و changes to ي and gets doubled to show strong quality
Added ة to show one specific evil action
Changed to plural form with ات and added ُ because it's doing the action of appearing in the verse
This is a connecting word that links to عَمِلُوا۟ (they did/worked). Together they mean 'that which they did'
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb
Added وا at the end to show a group of people did this action
The middle letter ي changes to ا and we add fatha marks to show this happened in the past

steps.m: and surrounded/encompassed (them in مَّا كَانُوا۟ بِهِۦ يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ)
steps.n: Adds و at the beginning to connect this with what came before. This word is connected to what comes after it - the thing that surrounded them was what they used to mock.
Basic preposition that shows how something is done or connected
Added هِم at the end to show this refers to 'them'. This word connects to يَسْتَهْزِءُونَ (they mock), showing what they were mocking with.
This is a relative pronoun that connects to the verb عَمِلُوا (they did), showing what they did
Added emphasis by doubling the م sound. This emphasizes the connection between what they did and its consequences.

steps.m: to be or exist (in a state or condition)

steps.n: The fatha mark shows this is a past tense verb
Adding the second fatha completes the past tense verb pattern

steps.m: they were (referring to those who mocked)
steps.n: The وا ending shows multiple people did this action. In this verse, it refers back to those who used to mock.
Added kasra to show this is a preposition meaning 'with'

steps.m: with him/it (referring to what they used to mock)
steps.n: Added ه with kasra to show this means 'him/it', referring back to what they used to mock in the verse

steps.m: with him/it (referring to what they used to mock)
steps.n: Added small alif (ۦ) after ه as a pronunciation guide to show the vowel is long

steps.m: to mock or make fun of something/someone

steps.n: Added vowels to connect the root letters and show this happened in the past
The است prefix shows someone is trying to do the action intensively or repeatedly
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: they mock/ridicule (referring to the disbelievers in وَحَاقَ بِهِم)
steps.n: Added ون to show multiple people are doing the action. This refers back to the disbelievers mentioned earlier in the verse.
Added vowels to make the basic past tense form. The middle letter و becomes ا when used in past tense.
Changed the vowels to show that something was done to the word (passive voice) rather than the word doing something. The middle letter ا changes to ي in passive voice.

steps.m: and it was said (connected to نَنسَىٰكُمْ - we will forget you)
steps.n: Added وَ at start to mean 'and'. This connects this statement to what follows in the ayah - 'and it was said... we will forget you'
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to make the basic noun form. The middle letter و stays as a consonant here.
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific

steps.m: the day (object of قِيلَ/it was said)
steps.n: Added َ at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb قِيلَ (it was said). Also, the initial اَلْ is written as ٱلْ when connecting words in the Quran.
Basic past tense verb meaning 'forgot'

steps.m: we forget (كَمَا نَسِيتُمْ - just as you forgot)
steps.n: Added ن at beginning to show 'we' are doing the action. The ي at the end changes to ى because it's a weak letter at the end.

steps.m: we will forget you all (كَمَا نَسِيتُمْ - just as you forgot)
steps.n: Added كُمْ at the end to show who is being forgotten. The alif (ٰ) is added to extend the sound.
Basic comparison particle that shows similarity

steps.m: just as (connects to نَسِيتُمْ - you forgot)
steps.n: Added مَا to make the comparison more specific and connect it to what follows. In this ayah, it connects to the verb نَسِيتُمْ (you forgot), showing that the forgetting is happening in the same way as something else.
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the basic past tense verb
Added تُ at the end to show 'I' did the action

steps.m: you all forgot (referring to the disbelievers being addressed in this ayah)
steps.n: Changed the ending to تُمْ to show that many people are being addressed
Adding vowel marks to show this is a past tense verb meaning 'he met'
Changed the verb into a noun that means the act of meeting
Added fatha at the end to show this word is being acted upon by the verb نَسِيتُمْ (you forgot)
Basic noun form meaning 'day'

steps.m: your (plural) day (object of نَنسَىٰكُمْ - we will forget you)
steps.n: Added ِكُمْ at the end where ِ shows possession and كُمْ means 'your (plural)'. This word is being acted upon by the verb نَنسَىٰكُمْ (we will forget you) in this ayah.
This is the basic demonstrative element that points to something

steps.m: this (pointing to something in the ayah)
steps.n: Added ذا to make it specifically point to something masculine and nearby. The small alif (ٰ) helps connect the sounds.
Added مَ at the start to show 'place where' something happens. The ى at the end is because the last root letter is weak.
Added كُمُ to show it belongs to multiple people being addressed

steps.m: and your (all) place of shelter/refuge (connected to نَنسَىٰكُمْ - We will forget you)
steps.n: Added وَ to connect this word to what came before it in the verse
Changed the root into a noun meaning 'fire' since fire gives light. The و (weak letter) is changed to ا.
Added damma (ُ) to show this is the subject of the sentence
Added ال (the) to make it 'the fire' instead of just 'fire'. The ُ (damma) shows it's doing the action as the subject.
Added fatha to show correct pronunciation
Added و with fatha at the beginning to connect this phrase to the previous statement. This word connects to نَّاصِرِينَ (helpers) in the ayah to say 'and you have no helpers'
Added fatha to show the vowel sound on the preposition ل
Added the pronoun كم (you all) to show who the preposition is directed to
Basic preposition meaning 'from'. In this ayah, it connects to نَّاصِرِينَ (helpers) to show 'from [among] helpers'
The dot under م disappears and a shadda is added because it connects smoothly to the next word نَّاصِرِينَ - like when you join words together while speaking
Added vowels to connect the letters together and show this happened in the past
Changed the word from an action (helped) to a person who does the action (helper)
Makes the word plural - now means multiple helpers instead of just one
Added the vowel mark َ (fatHa) at the end to show this word is being used as an object in the sentence
This is the basic demonstrative pronoun used to point to something nearby. Since it doesn't come from a root, we start with its simplest form.
Adding ل with a kasra (ِ) changes it from pointing to something near to pointing to something far away. The alif is lengthened with ٰ to show distance.
Adding كُم at the end shows this word is addressing multiple people (you all). This connects to the next ayah which talks about what these people did.
This is the base word that shows confirmation or emphasis
Adding بِ at the beginning changes 'that' to 'because' or 'due to'

steps.m: because you all (connects to اتَّخَذْتُمْ - you all took)
steps.n: Adding كُمُ at the end shows this applies to multiple people being spoken to. This connects to the next word اتَّخَذْتُمْ (you all took)
These are the three basic letters that give us the core meaning
Adding fathas connects the letters into a past tense verb
Adding ت with shadda and changing the first letter to ا with kasra makes the action reflexive - doing something for oneself
Adding تُ at the end and putting sukun on the last root letter shows 'you' or 'I' did the action
Adding م changes it from singular 'you' to plural 'you all'
The initial اِ is written as ٱ in the Quran, but the pronunciation stays the same

steps.m: to show clearly or make evident
steps.n: This root relates to things that are clear or evident
Adding ة makes this a feminine noun meaning 'sign'
Added ات to make it plural, showing multiple signs or verses
The word changed to show it belongs to Allah - it got a kasra (small diagonal line) at the end and uses a special long alif mark
Added ال and merged it with the word, changing إ to ل with shadda, making it refer specifically to Allah, the One True God
Added kasra at the end to show this word belongs to or describes something else. In this ayah, it shows these are 'the signs of Allah' that were taken in jest.

steps.m: to mock or make fun of
steps.n: This root relates to making fun of something
Adding the damma vowels changes this from a verb into a noun meaning 'mockery'
The hamza changes to و and tanween is added to show this is what was 'taken as' or 'made into' in the phrase 'you took God's signs as mockery'
The و means 'and'
Root letters turned into past tense verb with shadda making it stronger - 'he really deceived'
Added ت to show feminine subject (referring to الْحَيَوٰةُ ٱلدُّنْيَا - worldly life)
Added و to connect this clause with what came before
Added كُمُ to show who was deceived (you all)
Added vowels and changed one ي to و to form the abstract noun pattern. This pattern is used for concepts like 'life'.
Added ة at the end to change from general 'life' to a specific instance of life

steps.m: the life (worldly life in this ayah)
steps.n: Added ٱلْ at the beginning to make it definite and ُ at the end to show it's the subject. This word refers specifically to worldly life in this context, as shown by الدنيا after it.
The root letters are connected with damma on first letter, sukun on second, and the و changes to ي with an alif at the end to make a feminine adjective meaning 'lower' or 'worldly'
Added ال at the beginning to make it definite. The د gets a shadda because it comes after ال. This word describes 'the life' (ٱلْحَيَوٰةُ) in the verse.
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to form the basic noun. The و gets a sukun (ْ) because it's a weak letter.
Added the definite article ال to make it specific
Added فَ to show this follows from what was mentioned before

steps.m: so today (object of what happens next in يُخْرَجُونَ)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end to show this is the object of what happens. The alif in ال becomes waslah (ٱ) after فَ. This word connects to يُخْرَجُونَ (they are removed) in the next part, showing when they won't be removed.
This is a negative particle that negates the verb يُخْرَجُونَ (they are brought out) that comes after it in the ayah. It's like adding 'not' before a verb in English.
Added fatha mark to help with pronunciation
Adding fathas connects the letters and makes it a past tense verb
Adding يَ at start and changing vowels shows this happens in present/future

steps.m: he is made to exit/is expelled
steps.n: Changing the vowels makes it passive - showing the action is done to someone rather than them doing it

steps.m: they are made to exit/are expelled
steps.n: Adding ونَ makes it refer to a group rather than one person
Combination of من (from) and ها (it/her)
Basic negation particle that makes something negative. Here it negates the verb يُخْرَجُونَ (they are brought out), making it mean 'they are not brought out'.
